result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Diacritics
8: 
9: Diacritics let a letter have many variant. We only focus on letter diacritics in this section. Tones
10: have seperated section.
11: 
12: #### Empty
13: 
14: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
15: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
16:   characters.
17: 
18: #### Circumflex
19: 
20: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
21: 
22: #### Breve
23: 
24: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
25: 
26: #### Horn
27: 
28: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
29: 
30: #### Bar
31: 
32: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
33: 
34: #### Apply rules
35: 
36: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
37: - _Circumflex_, _bar_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
38:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
39:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
40:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
41: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
42:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
43:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
44:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
45: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
46: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
47: 
48: #### Override rules
49: 
50: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
51:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
52:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
53: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible letter diacritic.
54: - Keep tone untouched.
55: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
56: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
57: 
58: #### Cancel rules
59: 
60: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
61:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
62: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
63:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
64:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
65:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
66:   - Bar: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
67: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
68: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
69:   remains until the process is reset (usually when starting new word).
70:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
71: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
72: 
73: #### Auto-fill rules
74: 
75: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
76:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
77: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
78:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
79:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
80:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
81: - The new input character is appended literally.
82: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
83: 
84: ### Tones
85: 
86: _(the tones specification is not ready)_
87: 
88: #### Level
89: 
90: #### Rising
91: 
92: #### Falling
93: 
94: #### Dipping-rising
95: 
96: #### Rising-glottalized
97: 
98: #### Falling-glottalized
99: 
100: #### Apply rules
101: 
102: #### Override rules
103: 
104: #### Reposition rules

content.thinking: I need to look back at how the circumflex and horn diacritics are handled in the existing 'D'/'d' implementation to understand the pattern, and also check how the code manages cases where the buffer exceeds its effective limit.

I'm mapping out how to implement the bar diacritic rules for 'D'/'d', using the 'E'/'e' pattern as a template since both have a single diacritic option. The logic follows three cases: handling literals when the buffer is empty, applying the diacritic when the previous character matches the base, and managing state transitions when a diacritic is already present.

content.text:
## Plan: implement `'D', 'd'` branch (bar rules)

The `D`/`d` branch is structurally the closest to the `E`/`e` branch: a single diacritic (`bar`) with apply + cancel paths, no override (per spec, only `A` and `O` have override rules).

### Branches to implement (in order, mirroring the existing pattern)

1. **Literal / empty buffer guard** — when `literal_index != null`, `mode == .literal`, or `buffer_length == 0`: `append_literal(c)` and set `buffer_modification_index = null`.
2. **Apply bar** — previous span base matches (ignore case) and `diacritic == .empty`: rebuild previous span with `.bar`, keep tone, set `buffer_modification_index = self.buffer_length - 1`.
3. **Cancel bar** — previous span base matches (ignore case) and `diacritic == .bar`: rebuild previous span with `.empty`, keep tone, set `buffer_modification_index = self.buffer_length - 1`, set `literal_index = self.buffer_length`, then `append_literal(c)`.
4. **Different base — literal** — guarded by `std.ascii.toLower(prev.base) != std.ascii.toLower(c)`: if `buffer_length == self.buffer_effective.len` set `literal_index = self.buffer_length` (the explicit literal-exceeds-buffer_effective case, kept as a separate `if` exactly like in the `E`/`e` branch), then `append_literal(c)` and `buffer_modification_index = null`.
5. **`else => unreachable`** — closing the exhaustive ladder.

### Notes on conformance

- No override branch (spec §Override: only `A`, `O`).
- Tone is preserved by passing `span_previous.tone` into `Span.init_diacritic_tone` (matches existing pattern; safe even though `D` is a consonant since the existing test only seeds `.level`, and `Span.init_diacritic_tone` will accept `.level` for any base).
- Comments will follow the existing numbered-step style (`// 1.`, `// 2.`, `// 3.` …) as in the `E`/`e` block, with a tail `// ?` for the literal-different-base case where appropriate. Since `D` has only 4 cases, I will number them `1`–`4` and not use `?`.
- The literal-exceeds-`buffer_effective` `if` stays as a separate inner `if` inside case 4, exactly as you wrote it for `A`, `E`, `O`, `W`.
- The existing test at lines 757–787 will be the verification target. No new tests will be added.

Confirm and I will apply the edit.

My comments:

- use toUpper for consistency.

Let me preview the plan again.

## Plan: implement `'D', 'd'` branch (bar rules)

Mirror the `E`/`e` branch structure (single diacritic, apply + cancel, no override), but use `std.ascii.toUpper(...)` for case-insensitive base comparisons — matching the `W`/`w` branch convention.

### Branches (in order)

1. **Literal / empty buffer guard** — `literal_index != null`, `mode == .literal`, or `buffer_length == 0`: `append_literal(c)`, `buffer_modification_index = null`.
2. **Apply bar** — `std.ascii.toUpper(prev.base) == 'D'` and `prev.diacritic == .empty`: rebuild previous span with `.bar`, keep tone; `buffer_modification_index = self.buffer_length - 1`.
3. **Cancel bar** — `std.ascii.toUpper(prev.base) == 'D'` and `prev.diacritic == .bar`: rebuild previous span with `.empty`, keep tone; `buffer_modification_index = self.buffer_length - 1`; set `literal_index = self.buffer_length`; `append_literal(c)`.
4. **Different base — literal** — `std.ascii.toUpper(prev.base) != 'D'`: if `buffer_length == self.buffer_effective.len` set `literal_index = self.buffer_length` (separate inner `if`, same shape as in `A`/`E`/`O`/`W` branches); `append_literal(c)`; `buffer_modification_index = null`.
5. **`else => unreachable`**.

### Notes

- Comments follow numbered-step style `// 1.` … `// 4.` (no `// ?` since there are only 4 cases).
- Tone preserved via `span_previous.tone` passed to `Span.init_diacritic_tone`.
- Existing test at lines 757–787 is the verification target; no new tests added.

Confirm and I will apply.

My comments:

- change all char compare to use toUpper for consistency.

Implement the plan.

All 17 tests pass. `zig fmt` clean. No remaining `toLower` references.

Final state:
- `ascii_equal_ignore_case` helper restored, body switched from `toLower` to `toUpper`.
- A/E/O branches restored to use `ascii_equal_ignore_case`; their final `else if` literal-different-base check switched from `toLower(...) != toLower(c)` to `toUpper(...) != toUpper(c)`.
- New `D`/`d` branch follows the same pattern (helper for case-insensitive base+diacritic checks; `toUpper(...) != toUpper(c)` for the literal-different-base check).
Review all places that we can use `ascii_equal_ignore_case` instead of `toUpper` in the if conditions. Let me know them.

content.thinking: I'm searching for instances where `toUpper` is being used in conditional checks that could be replaced with `ascii_equal_ignore_case` for better performance.

content.text:
## Candidates for `ascii_equal_ignore_case` substitution

### Eligible (compares `prev.base` against `c`, both u8 alphabet)

These four `!= ` lines are the literal-different-base guard at the tail of the `A`/`E`/`O`/`D` branches. They could be rewritten as `!ascii_equal_ignore_case(prev.base, c)`:

- [src/liblex.zig:282](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L282) — `A`/`a` branch.
- [src/liblex.zig:321](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L321) — `D`/`d` branch.
- [src/liblex.zig:359](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L359) — `E`/`e` branch.
- [src/liblex.zig:408](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L408) — `O`/`o` branch.

Tradeoff: replacing `!=` with `!ascii_equal_ignore_case(...)` adds a logical-not and slightly hides the inequality that mirrors the earlier equality checks in the same `else if` ladder. Worth doing only if you prefer helper consistency over the explicit `!=` symmetry.

### Not eligible

These compare `prev.base` against a fixed letter literal (`'A'`, `'O'`, `'U'`), not against `c`. Since `'A'`, `'O'`, `'U'` are already uppercase, `ascii_equal_ignore_case(prev.base, 'A')` would technically work but it would obscure that the right-hand side is a hard-coded constant rather than the variable input. Recommend keeping these as-is:

- [src/liblex.zig:434](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L434), [:440](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L440), [:446](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L446) — `W`/`w` branch checking `'A'`.
- [src/liblex.zig:456](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L456), [:462](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L462), [:468](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L468) — `W`/`w` branch checking `'O'`.
- [src/liblex.zig:478](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L478), [:484](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L484) — `W`/`w` branch checking `'U'`.
- [src/liblex.zig:494](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L494) — `std.mem.indexOfScalar(u8, "AOU", ...)`; the `toUpper` is needed for the lookup, no helper applicable.

Confirm whether to apply the substitution to the four eligible lines.
Yes. I agree with you. Apply the change.
